title: The brain — structure and function
examTip: AQA regularly asks you to name a brain region and state its function — and they also ask a discussion question about why studying the brain is difficult. Both are very predictable. Know three regions and three functions cold, then have a clear explanation of why brain research is hard.
introTips: You're going to learn which parts of the brain do what — and why we don't fully understand it yet, despite all of modern medicine.
Three regions come up in AQA exams again and again: the cerebral cortex, cerebellum, and medulla oblongata. Know each one's function without hesitating.
The 'difficulties of studying the brain' is a 3–4 mark question AQA loves to include — it's worth preparing a clear, structured answer for it.
intro: The brain is arguably the most complex object in the known universe — and it controls everything you do, consciously or not. From recognising a face to keeping your heart beating while you sleep, it's all coordinated by this 1.4 kg mass of neurones. Scientists have been studying it for centuries and still don't fully understand it, which is why it features heavily in AQA's 'required knowledge' and their 'evaluate the evidence' style questions. This lesson covers the key regions, what they control, and why studying the brain remains so challenging.

keyPoints.heading: The cerebral cortex — conscious thought and personality
keyPoints.content: The cerebral cortex is the large, folded outer layer of the brain and is responsible for the functions that make us distinctly human. It controls conscious thought, memory, language, personality, and intelligence. It also processes sensory information from your eyes, ears, and skin, and coordinates voluntary movement. The folded (convoluted) surface increases its surface area, allowing more neurones to fit within the skull. It is the most recently evolved part of the brain, and damage to specific regions of the cortex causes very specific losses of function — which is how scientists have mapped which area does what.
writeBullets: Cerebral cortex = outer, folded layer of the brain
Controls: conscious thought, memory, language, personality, intelligence
Processes sensory information and coordinates voluntary movement
Folded surface = larger surface area = more neurones
Damage to specific cortex regions causes specific losses of function

keyTerms.def: The large, folded outer layer of the brain responsible for conscious thought, memory, language, and personality
Deliberate, aware mental activity — coordinated by the cerebral cortex

keyPoints.memTip: Cortex = Conscious. The 'C' in cerebral cortex stands for Conscious, Complex, Creative, Clever. Everything that makes you you is here. Memory trick: 'The cortex makes you complex.'

keyPoints.heading: The cerebellum and medulla oblongata
keyPoints.content: The cerebellum is at the back and bottom of the brain. It coordinates balance, posture, and fine motor control — it's what allows you to walk, catch a ball, or play an instrument without consciously thinking through every tiny muscle movement. Damage to the cerebellum causes loss of coordination and balance. The medulla oblongata is at the base of the brain, connecting it to the spinal cord. It controls unconscious, automatic functions that keep you alive: heart rate, breathing rate, and blood pressure. These functions happen continuously without any conscious input — if the medulla is damaged, they stop.
writeBullets: Cerebellum: coordinates balance, posture, and fine motor control
Cerebellum damage → loss of coordination (e.g. difficulty walking straight)
Medulla oblongata: controls unconscious automatic functions
Medulla controls: heart rate, breathing rate, blood pressure
Medulla damage → loss of automatic life-sustaining functions

keyTerms.def: The part of the brain that coordinates balance, posture, and fine motor control
The part of the brain that controls unconscious, automatic functions such as heart rate and breathing

keyPoints.memTip: Cerebellum = Balance and coordination. Medulla = automatic functions (heartbeat, breathing). Two memory phrases: 'The cerebeLLum makes you beLAnced.' 'The medulla keeps you aLIVE without thinking.' These two get confused in exams — fix them separately.

keyPoints.heading: Why studying the brain is difficult
keyPoints.content: Despite centuries of research, the brain remains poorly understood. There are several key reasons. First, ethical constraints: deliberately damaging or interfering with a healthy human brain to study it is not acceptable, so controlled experiments are limited. Second, complexity: the brain contains approximately 86 billion neurones, each forming thousands of connections — the sheer number of interactions makes it almost impossible to map fully. Third, individual variation: no two brains are identical, making it hard to generalise findings. Researchers rely mainly on non-invasive techniques such as MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) and CT scans to observe brain structure and activity without causing harm. They also study people with brain injuries — like the famous case of Phineas Gage, who survived a metal rod passing through his frontal lobe, revealing which regions control personality.
writeBullets: Ethical constraints: cannot deliberately damage healthy brains for experiments
Complexity: ~86 billion neurones with thousands of connections each
Individual variation: no two brains are identical
Non-invasive techniques used: MRI and CT scans
Brain injury case studies reveal region function (e.g. Phineas Gage — frontal lobe → personality)
Limited experimental controls make conclusions harder to verify

keyTerms.def: A non-invasive scanning technique that produces detailed images of brain structure and activity
A non-invasive X-ray based scan that produces cross-sectional images of the brain
Detailed study of an individual — brain injury cases help reveal which regions control which functions

keyPoints.memTip: Three reasons the brain is hard to study: Ethics, Complexity, Variation — ECV. Then add: scientists use MRI/CT and brain injury cases to get around these limitations. Write ECV, then the two workarounds, as a short bullet list from memory.

tables.title: Key brain regions and their functions
rows: Loss of specific thought or sensory processing depending on area damaged
Unconscious control of heart rate and breathing

0.question: Which region of the brain controls conscious thought, memory, and language?
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — the cerebral cortex is responsible for conscious thought, memory, language, and personality. It's the large, folded outer layer.
0.wrongFeedback: The cerebral cortex controls conscious thought, memory, and language. The medulla oblongata controls automatic functions like heart rate and breathing. The cerebellum controls balance and coordination.

1.question: A patient suffers a brain injury and afterwards has difficulty maintaining balance and coordinating movements. Which region was most likely damaged?
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — the cerebellum coordinates balance and fine motor control. Damage there causes exactly this kind of difficulty.
1.wrongFeedback: Balance and coordination are controlled by the cerebellum. The cerebral cortex deals with conscious thought; the medulla oblongata controls heart rate and breathing. Remember: cerebellum = balance.

2.question: Which of the following best explains why it is difficult to study the brain?
options: The brain is too small to examine with modern equipment
Scientists are not interested in brain research
It is unethical to deliberately interfere with healthy human brains, and the brain is extremely complex with individual variation between people
Brain scans have not yet been invented
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly — ethical constraints, complexity, and individual variation are the three main reasons. Plus, the non-invasive techniques we do have (MRI, CT) give limited insight into function.
2.wrongFeedback: The main difficulties are ethical (can't experiment on healthy brains), complexity (billions of neurones with thousands of connections), and individual variation. MRI and CT scans exist but are non-invasive and have limitations.

commonMistakes: Confusing the cerebellum with the cerebral cortex — the cerebellum controls balance and coordination, not thought. The cerebral cortex handles conscious functions. Mix these up and question marks disappear.
Forgetting the medulla oblongata controls unconscious functions — students often say 'the brain controls breathing' without specifying the medulla. Examiners want the specific region.
Not giving enough reasons when explaining why studying the brain is difficult — one reason is rarely enough for a 3-mark question. Practise giving three: ethics, complexity, individual variation.
Saying 'brain scans' without naming the technique — use 'MRI' or 'CT scan' specifically. Vague answers don't earn full marks.
Confusing correlation with causation in brain studies — brain injury cases show association between a region and a function, but they don't always prove direct cause and effect.
examTips: Name the region and state its function in the same sentence — 'the cerebellum coordinates balance and fine motor control'. AQA mark schemes look for both pieces together.

For 'difficulties of studying the brain' questions, aim for three distinct points: (1) ethical issues with experimentation, (2) extreme complexity of neural connections, (3) individual variation. Add non-invasive techniques as your 'scientists' response to these difficulties.
When discussing brain injury case studies, explain what they tell us AND their limitations — conclusions from one individual may not generalise to the whole population.
AQA sometimes asks whether neuroscience findings are reliable — mentioning small sample sizes, ethical limitations, and individual variation earns evaluation marks.
revisionCardBullets: Cerebral cortex: conscious thought, memory, language, personality — large folded outer layer
Cerebellum: balance, posture, fine motor coordination — at the back of the brain
Medulla oblongata: unconscious automatic functions — heart rate, breathing rate
Brain is difficult to study: ethical constraints, extreme complexity, individual variation
Non-invasive techniques: MRI and CT scans allow brain structure/activity to be observed without harm
Brain injury case studies reveal region function (e.g. Phineas Gage → frontal lobe → personality)
Cerebral cortex damage → specific loss of thought/sensory function depending on area
Cerebellum damage → loss of coordination and balance
Medulla damage → failure of automatic life-sustaining functions
~86 billion neurones in the human brain — each connected to thousands of others
